kids.question: Who baptized Jesus in the river Jordan?
kids.explanation: Mark 1:9 — "Jesus came from Nazareth of Galilee, and was baptized of John in Jordan."

kids.question: What happened when Jesus came up out of the water?
kids.option_a: The heavens opened and the Spirit descended like a dove
kids.explanation: Mark 1:10 — "And straightway coming up out of the water, he saw the heavens opened, and the Spirit like a dove descending upon him."

kids.question: What did Jesus tell the fishermen to follow Him and He would make them?
kids.explanation: Mark 1:17 — "Come ye after me, and I will make you to become fishers of men."

teens.question: What Old Testament prophet is quoted at the beginning of Mark?
teens.option_a: Isaiah
teens.explanation: Mark 1:2-3 — "As it is written in the prophets... The voice of one crying in the wilderness, Prepare ye the way of the Lord" (quoting Isaiah 40:3).

teens.question: How long was Jesus tempted in the wilderness?
teens.option_a: Forty days
teens.explanation: Mark 1:13 — "And he was there in the wilderness forty days, tempted of Satan."

teens.question: What was the first thing Jesus preached?
teens.option_a: The kingdom of God is at hand: repent ye, and believe the gospel
teens.option_d: Blessed are the poor in spirit
teens.explanation: Mark 1:15 — "The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God is at hand: repent ye, and believe the gospel."

teens.question: What did the unclean spirit say to Jesus in the synagogue at Capernaum?
teens.option_a: I know thee who thou art, the Holy One of God
teens.explanation: Mark 1:24 — "I know thee who thou art, the Holy One of God."

teens.question: What did Jesus do very early in the morning after healing many people?
teens.option_a: Rose up a great while before day and went to a solitary place to pray
teens.explanation: Mark 1:35 — "And in the morning, rising up a great while before day, he went out, and departed into a solitary place, and there prayed."

adults.question: What is the very first verse of Mark's Gospel?
adults.option_a: The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, the Son of God
adults.option_b: In the beginning was the Word
adults.option_c: The book of the generation of Jesus Christ
adults.option_d: Forasmuch as many have taken in hand
adults.explanation: Mark 1:1 — "The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, the Son of God."

adults.question: What did John the Baptist wear and eat according to Mark?
adults.option_a: Camel's hair with a leather girdle; locusts and wild honey
adults.option_b: Fine linen and bread
adults.option_c: Wool and fish
adults.option_d: Sackcloth and figs
adults.explanation: Mark 1:6 — "And John was clothed with camel's hair, and with a girdle of a skin about his loins; and he did eat locusts and wild honey."

adults.question: What voice spoke from heaven at Jesus' baptism and what did it say?
adults.option_a: Thou art my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased
adults.option_b: This is a great prophet, listen to him
adults.option_c: Behold the Lamb of God
adults.option_d: Go and sin no more
adults.explanation: Mark 1:11 — "And there came a voice from heaven, saying, Thou art my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased."

adults.question: Which two sets of brothers did Jesus call as His first four disciples?
adults.option_a: Simon and Andrew, then James and John (sons of Zebedee)
adults.option_b: Philip and Bartholomew, then Thomas and Matthew
adults.option_c: James and Judas, then Peter and Andrew
adults.option_d: Matthew and Thomas, then Philip and Nathanael
adults.explanation: Mark 1:16-20 — Simon and Andrew casting a net, then James and John mending their nets.

adults.question: What did Jesus command the unclean spirit, and what happened?
adults.option_a: He rebuked him saying "Hold thy peace, and come out of him," and the spirit came out with convulsions and a loud cry
adults.option_b: He touched the man and the spirit vanished silently
adults.option_c: He prayed over the man for many hours
adults.option_d: He sent the man to the priests for cleansing
adults.explanation: Mark 1:25-26 — "Jesus rebuked him, saying, Hold thy peace, and come out of him. And when the unclean spirit had torn him, and cried with a loud voice, he came out of him."

adults.question: Whom did Jesus heal in Simon's house, and how?
adults.option_a: Simon's wife's mother, sick of a fever; He took her by the hand and lifted her up
adults.option_b: Simon's daughter who was lame; He spoke a word
adults.option_c: Simon's servant who was blind; He touched his eyes
adults.option_d: Simon's brother who had leprosy; He commanded him to wash
adults.explanation: Mark 1:30-31 — "But Simon's wife's mother lay sick of a fever... and he came and took her by the hand, and lifted her up; and immediately the fever left her."

adults.question: What did Jesus tell the cleansed leper NOT to do, and what did the leper do?
adults.option_a: Jesus charged him to say nothing, but the man published it much and blazed abroad the matter
adults.option_b: Jesus told him not to return home, but he went home immediately
adults.option_c: Jesus told him to stay in the city, but he fled to the wilderness
adults.option_d: Jesus told him to go to Jerusalem, but he went to Samaria
adults.explanation: Mark 1:44-45 — "See thou say nothing to any man... But he went out, and began to publish it much, and to blaze abroad the matter."

adults.question: What theological significance does Mark's use of "straightway" (euthys) throughout chapter 1 convey?
adults.option_a: It emphasizes the urgency and immediate authority of Jesus' ministry as the active Servant of God
adults.option_b: It shows that Mark was writing in a hurry
adults.option_c: It indicates the events happened on a single day
adults.option_d: It suggests Mark had poor literary style
adults.explanation: Mark uses "straightway/immediately" over 40 times in his Gospel, emphasizing Christ's decisive action — especially prominent in chapter 1 (vv. 10, 12, 18, 20, 21, 23, 28, 29, 30, 42).

kids.question: How did the sick man's friends get him to Jesus?
kids.option_a: They lowered him through the roof on his bed
kids.option_b: They carried him through the front door
kids.option_c: They waited outside until Jesus came out
kids.option_d: They called Jesus on a phone
kids.explanation: Mark 2:4 — "They uncovered the roof where he was: and when they had broken it up, they let down the bed wherein the sick of the palsy lay."

kids.question: What did Jesus tell the sick man to do?
kids.option_a: Arise, take up thy bed, and walk
kids.explanation: Mark 2:11 — "I say unto thee, Arise, and take up thy bed, and go thy way into thine house."

kids.question: Who did Jesus call to follow Him from the tax collector's table?
kids.explanation: Mark 2:14 — "And as he passed by, he saw Levi the son of Alphaeus sitting at the receipt of custom, and said unto him, Follow me."

teens.question: What did Jesus say first to the paralyzed man, and why did the scribes object?
teens.option_a: He said "Thy sins be forgiven thee," and the scribes said only God can forgive sins
teens.option_b: He said "Stand up and walk," and the scribes said he broke the Sabbath
teens.option_c: He said "Your faith has saved you," and the scribes questioned his authority
teens.option_d: He said "Be healed," and the scribes doubted his power
teens.explanation: Mark 2:5,7 — "Son, thy sins be forgiven thee... Why doth this man thus speak blasphemies? who can forgive sins but God only?"

teens.question: Why did the Pharisees criticize Jesus for eating with publicans and sinners?
teens.option_a: They believed a righteous teacher should not associate with sinful people
teens.option_b: They were concerned about food safety
teens.option_c: They wanted a private dinner invitation
teens.option_d: They objected to the type of food served
teens.explanation: Mark 2:16 — "How is it that he eateth and drinketh with publicans and sinners?"

teens.question: How did Jesus respond to the criticism about eating with sinners?
teens.option_a: They that are whole have no need of the physician, but they that are sick
teens.option_b: He apologized and left the table
teens.option_d: He told the sinners to leave
teens.explanation: Mark 2:17 — "They that are whole have no need of the physician, but they that are sick: I came not to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance."

teens.question: What illustration did Jesus use about new wine?
teens.option_a: New wine must be put into new bottles (wineskins)
teens.option_b: New wine should be poured onto the ground
teens.option_c: New wine must be mixed with old wine
teens.option_d: New wine should be stored in clay pots
teens.explanation: Mark 2:22 — "And no man putteth new wine into old bottles... but new wine must be put into new bottles."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about the Sabbath in response to the Pharisees?
teens.option_a: The sabbath was made for man, and not man for the sabbath
teens.option_c: The sabbath rules must be made stricter
teens.option_d: The sabbath only applies to priests
teens.explanation: Mark 2:27 — "The sabbath was made for man, and not man for the sabbath."

adults.question: What does Jesus' statement "Son, thy sins be forgiven thee" reveal about the relationship between sin and sickness?
adults.option_a: Jesus addresses the root spiritual need before the physical, demonstrating His divine authority to forgive sins
adults.option_b: That all sickness is directly caused by personal sin
adults.option_c: That physical healing is unimportant
adults.option_d: That forgiveness requires a temple sacrifice
adults.explanation: Mark 2:5 — "Son, thy sins be forgiven thee." Jesus prioritizes the man's spiritual condition, then heals physically to prove His authority (v. 10).

adults.question: What reasoning did Jesus use to prove He had power to forgive sins?
adults.option_a: Whether is it easier to say, Thy sins be forgiven; or to say, Arise and walk? — then He healed the man as visible proof
adults.option_b: He quoted an Old Testament prophecy
adults.option_c: He called angels to testify
adults.option_d: He asked the Pharisees to verify His identity
adults.explanation: Mark 2:9-11 — "Whether is it easier to say to the sick of the palsy, Thy sins be forgiven thee; or to say, Arise, and take up thy bed, and walk? But that ye may know that the Son of man hath power on earth to forgive sins..."

adults.question: What title does Jesus use for Himself in verse 10, and what does it signify?
adults.option_a: The Son of man — a Messianic title from Daniel 7:13 indicating both divine authority and human identification
adults.option_b: The Son of David — referring only to His royal lineage
adults.option_c: The Son of God — emphasizing only His deity
adults.option_d: The Lamb of God — pointing to His sacrifice
adults.explanation: Mark 2:10 — "That ye may know that the Son of man hath power on earth to forgive sins." "Son of man" echoes Daniel 7:13-14.

adults.question: What Old Testament example did Jesus cite when defending His disciples for plucking grain on the Sabbath?
adults.option_a: David eating the shewbread in the house of God when Abiathar was high priest
adults.option_b: Moses gathering manna on the Sabbath
adults.option_c: Elijah being fed by ravens
adults.option_d: Joshua marching around Jericho on the Sabbath
adults.explanation: Mark 2:25-26 — "Have ye never read what David did... how he went into the house of God in the days of Abiathar the high priest, and did eat the shewbread."

adults.question: What does Jesus claim about Himself in Mark 2:28?
adults.option_a: The Son of man is Lord also of the sabbath
adults.option_b: The sabbath belongs to the Pharisees
adults.option_c: Only priests have authority over the sabbath
adults.option_d: The sabbath is no longer relevant
adults.explanation: Mark 2:28 — "Therefore the Son of man is Lord also of the sabbath."

adults.question: What do the parables of the unshrunk cloth and new wine teach about Jesus' ministry?
adults.option_a: Christ's new covenant cannot simply be patched onto old legalistic traditions; it requires a new framework
adults.option_b: Old traditions are always better than new ones
adults.option_c: Wine making was an important first-century industry
adults.option_d: Clothing repair was a common occupation
adults.explanation: Mark 2:21-22 — The new cloth and new wine illustrations show that Jesus' teaching is not merely a reform of Pharisaic Judaism but something entirely new.

adults.question: How does the calling of Levi illustrate the nature of Jesus' ministry?
adults.option_a: It shows Jesus deliberately chose a despised tax collector, demonstrating that His kingdom includes social outcasts
adults.option_b: It shows Jesus needed wealthy supporters
adults.option_c: It demonstrates that tax collectors were respected
adults.option_d: It proves that all vocations are equally moral
adults.explanation: Mark 2:14-15 — Jesus called Levi the publican and ate at his house with many sinners, scandalizing the religious establishment.

adults.question: What does the reaction of the crowd in verse 12 — "We never saw it on this fashion" — indicate about the healing?
adults.option_a: It was an unprecedented display of divine power that amazed all, including skeptics, and glorified God
adults.option_b: The crowd was simply entertained
adults.option_c: They had never seen a paralyzed man before
adults.option_d: They were frightened and wanted to flee
adults.explanation: Mark 2:12 — "Insomuch that they were all amazed, and glorified God, saying, We never saw it on this fashion."

kids.question: What was wrong with the man's hand in the synagogue?
kids.explanation: Mark 3:1 — "And there was a man there which had a withered hand."

kids.question: How many special helpers (apostles) did Jesus choose?
kids.explanation: Mark 3:14 — "And he ordained twelve, that they should be with him."

kids.question: Who did Jesus say are His brother, sister, and mother?
kids.option_a: Whoever does the will of God
kids.explanation: Mark 3:35 — "For whosoever shall do the will of God, the same is my brother, and my sister, and mother."

teens.question: Why were the Pharisees watching Jesus in the synagogue on the Sabbath?
teens.option_a: To see if He would heal on the sabbath so they could accuse Him
teens.option_c: To invite Him to a feast
teens.option_d: To ask Him questions about Moses
teens.explanation: Mark 3:2 — "And they watched him, whether he would heal him on the sabbath day; that they might accuse him."

teens.question: How did Jesus feel when He looked at the Pharisees before healing the man?
teens.option_a: He looked on them with anger, grieved at the hardness of their hearts
teens.option_c: He was amused by their questions
teens.option_d: He was frightened by their power
teens.explanation: Mark 3:5 — "And when he had looked round about on them with anger, being grieved for the hardness of their hearts."

teens.question: Who did the Pharisees join with to plot against Jesus?
teens.option_a: The Herodians
teens.explanation: Mark 3:6 — "And the Pharisees went forth, and straightway took counsel with the Herodians against him, how they might destroy him."

teens.question: What two purposes did Jesus ordain the twelve apostles for?
teens.option_a: To be with Him, and to be sent out to preach with power to heal and cast out devils
teens.option_b: To guard the temple and collect tithes
teens.option_c: To write the Old Testament and teach children
teens.option_d: To serve in the Roman government and spread news
teens.explanation: Mark 3:14-15 — "He ordained twelve, that they should be with him, and that he might send them forth to preach, And to have power to heal sicknesses, and to cast out devils."

teens.question: What unforgivable sin did Jesus warn about?
teens.option_a: Blasphemy against the Holy Ghost
teens.explanation: Mark 3:29 — "But he that shall blaspheme against the Holy Ghost hath never forgiveness, but is in danger of eternal damnation."

adults.question: What question did Jesus ask in the synagogue before healing the withered hand?
adults.option_a: Is it lawful to do good on the sabbath days, or to do evil? to save life, or to kill?
adults.option_b: Is it lawful to eat on the sabbath?
adults.option_c: Is it lawful to walk on the sabbath?
adults.option_d: Is it lawful to pray on the sabbath?
adults.explanation: Mark 3:4 — "Is it lawful to do good on the sabbath days, or to do evil? to save life, or to kill? But they held their peace."

adults.question: What does Mark record about the unclean spirits' response when they saw Jesus?
adults.option_a: They fell down before Him and cried, Thou art the Son of God
adults.option_b: They fled without speaking
adults.option_c: They challenged Him to prove His identity
adults.option_d: They ignored Him completely
adults.explanation: Mark 3:11 — "And unclean spirits, when they saw him, fell down before him, and cried, saying, Thou art the Son of God."

adults.question: What surname did Jesus give to James and John, and what does it mean?
adults.option_a: Boanerges, which is The sons of thunder
adults.option_b: Cephas, which is A stone
adults.option_c: Didymus, which is A twin
adults.option_d: Iscariot, which is Man of Kerioth
adults.explanation: Mark 3:17 — "And James the son of Zebedee, and John the brother of James; and he surnamed them Boanerges, which is, The sons of thunder."

adults.question: What did the scribes from Jerusalem accuse Jesus of?
adults.option_a: Having Beelzebub and casting out devils by the prince of the devils
adults.option_b: Being a drunkard and a glutton
adults.option_c: Stealing from the temple treasury
adults.option_d: Practicing Egyptian sorcery
adults.explanation: Mark 3:22 — "And the scribes which came down from Jerusalem said, He hath Beelzebub, and by the prince of the devils casteth he out devils."

adults.question: How did Jesus refute the accusation that He cast out demons by Satan's power?
adults.option_a: A kingdom divided against itself cannot stand; Satan would not cast out his own forces
adults.option_b: He performed an even greater miracle
adults.option_c: He quoted a passage from Leviticus
adults.option_d: He called witnesses to testify
adults.explanation: Mark 3:23-26 — "How can Satan cast out Satan? And if a kingdom be divided against itself, that kingdom cannot stand."

adults.question: What parable does Jesus use about the "strong man" in verse 27?
adults.option_a: No man can enter a strong man's house and spoil his goods unless he first bind the strong man
adults.option_b: A strong man builds his house on rock
adults.option_c: A strong man lifts heavy burdens for others
adults.option_d: A strong man guards the city gates
adults.explanation: Mark 3:27 — "No man can enter into a strong man's house, and spoil his goods, except he will first bind the strong man; and then he will spoil his house."

adults.question: Why specifically did Jesus say the scribes were guilty of blaspheming the Holy Ghost?
adults.option_a: Because they said He had an unclean spirit — attributing the Spirit's work to Satan
adults.option_b: Because they refused to attend the synagogue
adults.option_c: Because they did not fast on the appointed days
adults.option_d: Because they collected taxes for Rome
adults.explanation: Mark 3:30 — "Because they said, He hath an unclean spirit." They attributed the Holy Spirit's miraculous work through Christ to demonic power.

adults.question: What does Jesus' redefinition of family in verses 33-35 teach about the nature of the kingdom of God?
adults.option_a: Spiritual kinship through obedience to God's will transcends and supersedes biological family ties
adults.option_b: Biological family is unimportant in every situation
adults.option_c: Only celibate people can be part of God's family
adults.option_d: Family relationships are meaningless in the afterlife
adults.explanation: Mark 3:33-35 — "Who is my mother, or my brethren?... For whosoever shall do the will of God, the same is my brother, and my sister, and mother."

kids.question: In Jesus' story, what happened to the seed that fell on good ground?
kids.option_a: It grew and brought forth fruit, some thirty, sixty, and a hundredfold
kids.explanation: Mark 4:8 — "And other fell on good ground, and did yield fruit that sprang up and increased; and brought forth, some thirty, and some sixty, and some an hundred."

kids.question: What was Jesus doing in the boat during the storm?
kids.explanation: Mark 4:38 — "And he was in the hinder part of the ship, asleep on a pillow."

kids.question: What did Jesus say to make the storm stop?
kids.explanation: Mark 4:39 — "And he arose, and rebuked the wind, and said unto the sea, Peace, be still. And the wind ceased, and there was a great calm."

teens.question: In the parable of the sower, what do the thorns represent?
teens.option_a: The cares of this world, the deceitfulness of riches, and the lusts of other things
teens.explanation: Mark 4:19 — "And the cares of this world, and the deceitfulness of riches, and the lusts of other things entering in, choke the word, and it becometh unfruitful."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about hidden things in verse 22?
teens.option_a: There is nothing hid which shall not be manifested, nor kept secret but that it shall come abroad
teens.option_b: Some secrets will remain forever hidden
teens.option_c: Only God knows all hidden things
teens.explanation: Mark 4:22 — "For there is nothing hid, which shall not be manifested; neither was any thing kept secret, but that it should come abroad."

teens.question: What is the mustard seed parable about?
teens.option_a: The kingdom of God starts very small but grows to become the greatest
teens.explanation: Mark 4:31-32 — "When it is sown in the earth, is less than all the seeds... But when it is sown, it groweth up, and becometh greater than all herbs."

teens.question: What did the disciples say to Jesus during the storm?
teens.option_a: Master, carest thou not that we perish?
teens.option_b: Lord, save us, we are drowning
teens.option_c: Teacher, help us row to shore
teens.explanation: Mark 4:38 — "And they awake him, and say unto him, Master, carest thou not that we perish?"

teens.question: What question did the disciples ask each other after the storm was calmed?
teens.option_a: What manner of man is this, that even the wind and the sea obey him?
teens.option_b: How did he learn to control weather?
teens.option_c: Should we tell people about this?
teens.explanation: Mark 4:41 — "And they feared exceedingly, and said one to another, What manner of man is this, that even the wind and the sea obey him?"

adults.question: In the parable of the sower, what does the seed that fell by the way side represent?
adults.option_a: Those who hear the word but Satan immediately takes it away
adults.option_b: Those who hear and believe permanently
adults.option_c: Those who never hear the word
adults.option_d: Those who spread the word to others
adults.explanation: Mark 4:15 — "And these are they by the way side, where the word is sown; but when they have heard, Satan cometh immediately, and taketh away the word."

adults.question: What does Jesus explain about why He speaks in parables?
adults.option_a: That seeing they may see, and not perceive; and hearing they may hear, and not understand — fulfilling Isaiah's prophecy
adults.option_b: To make His teachings easier to understand
adults.option_c: To entertain the crowd
adults.option_d: To avoid controversy
adults.explanation: Mark 4:12 — "That seeing they may see, and not perceive; and hearing they may hear, and not understand; lest at any time they should be converted, and their sins should be forgiven them."

adults.question: What does the stony ground represent in the parable of the sower?
adults.option_a: Those who receive the word gladly but have no root and fall away when affliction or persecution arises
adults.option_b: Those who never hear the gospel
adults.option_c: Those who bear abundant fruit
adults.option_d: Those who deliberately reject the truth
adults.explanation: Mark 4:16-17 — "These are they... which, when they have heard the word, immediately receive it with gladness; And have no root... when affliction or persecution ariseth for the word's sake, immediately they are offended."

adults.question: What principle does Jesus state in verse 24 about measure?
adults.option_a: With what measure ye mete, it shall be measured to you: and unto you that hear shall more be given
adults.option_b: Only the wealthy will receive from God
adults.option_c: Everyone receives the same regardless
adults.option_d: God does not measure our deeds
adults.explanation: Mark 4:24 — "With what measure ye mete, it shall be measured to you: and unto you that hear shall more be given."

adults.question: In the parable of the seed growing secretly (Mark 4:26-29), what is the main point?
adults.option_a: The kingdom of God grows by divine power, not human effort — the earth bringeth forth fruit of herself
adults.option_b: Farmers should sleep more and work less
adults.option_c: Agriculture is the most godly profession
adults.option_d: Seeds are a symbol of money
adults.explanation: Mark 4:27-28 — "And should sleep, and rise night and day, and the seed should spring and grow up, he knoweth not how. For the earth bringeth forth fruit of herself."

adults.question: What does Jesus' rebuke "Why are ye so fearful? how is it that ye have no faith?" reveal?
adults.option_a: That faith and fear are incompatible — the disciples should have trusted Christ's presence even in the storm
adults.option_b: That storms are illusions
adults.option_c: That the disciples were cowards
adults.option_d: That Jesus expected them to calm the storm themselves
adults.explanation: Mark 4:40 — "And he said unto them, Why are ye so fearful? how is it that ye have no faith?"

adults.question: What does the calming of the storm demonstrate about Christ's nature?
adults.option_a: His divine sovereignty over creation, since only God commands wind and sea (cf. Psalm 107:29)
adults.option_b: He had learned weather control from the Essenes
adults.option_c: He was merely lucky with the timing
adults.option_d: He used a natural phenomenon
adults.explanation: Mark 4:39,41 — "Peace, be still... What manner of man is this, that even the wind and the sea obey him?" This parallels Psalm 107:29 — "He maketh the storm a calm."

adults.question: What literary function does Mark 4:33-34 serve regarding Jesus' teaching method?
adults.option_a: It summarizes that Jesus used many parables publicly but explained all things privately to His disciples, showing two levels of revelation
adults.option_b: It proves Jesus told only one parable
adults.option_c: It shows Jesus refused to teach the crowds
adults.option_d: It indicates the disciples never understood
adults.explanation: Mark 4:33-34 — "And with many such parables spake he the word unto them, as they were able to hear it. But without a parable spake he not unto them: and when they were alone, he expounded all things to his disciples."

kids.question: Where did the demons go when Jesus cast them out of the man?
kids.option_a: Into a herd of pigs that ran into the sea
kids.explanation: Mark 5:13 — "And the unclean spirits went out, and entered into the swine: and the herd ran violently down a steep place into the sea."

kids.question: What happened when the sick woman touched Jesus' clothes?
kids.explanation: Mark 5:29 — "And straightway the fountain of her blood was dried up; and she felt in her body that she was healed of that plague."

kids.question: What did Jesus say to the little girl who had died?
kids.option_a: Talitha cumi, which means Little girl, I say unto thee, arise
kids.explanation: Mark 5:41 — "And he took the damsel by the hand, and said unto her, Talitha cumi; which is, being interpreted, Damsel, I say unto thee, arise."

teens.question: What was the name the demon-possessed man gave when Jesus asked?
teens.option_a: Legion, because they were many
teens.explanation: Mark 5:9 — "And he asked him, What is thy name? And he answered, saying, My name is Legion: for we are many."

teens.question: What did the people of the region ask Jesus to do after He healed the man?
teens.option_a: They asked Him to depart out of their coasts
teens.option_b: They threw a feast for Him
teens.option_c: They asked Him to stay forever
teens.explanation: Mark 5:17 — "And they began to pray him to depart out of their coasts."

teens.question: What did Jesus tell the healed man to do?
teens.option_a: Go home to thy friends and tell them how great things the Lord hath done for thee
teens.option_b: Come and follow Me as a disciple
teens.option_c: Go to the temple and offer a sacrifice
teens.explanation: Mark 5:19 — "Go home to thy friends, and tell them how great things the Lord hath done for thee, and hath had compassion on thee."

teens.question: Who came to Jesus begging Him to heal his dying daughter?
teens.option_a: Jairus, a ruler of the synagogue
teens.explanation: Mark 5:22-23 — "And, behold, there cometh one of the rulers of the synagogue, Jairus by name... and besought him greatly, saying, My little daughter lieth at the point of death."

teens.question: What did Jesus say to Jairus when told his daughter was dead?
teens.option_a: Be not afraid, only believe
teens.option_d: Let us pray for her soul
teens.explanation: Mark 5:36 — "As soon as Jesus heard the word that was spoken, he saith unto the ruler of the synagogue, Be not afraid, only believe."

adults.question: How does Mark describe the demon-possessed man of the Gadarenes before Jesus healed him?
adults.option_a: He lived among the tombs, could not be bound even with chains, and cut himself with stones night and day
adults.option_b: He lived in a fine house and appeared normal
adults.option_c: He was mute and could not speak
adults.option_d: He was blind and deaf from birth
adults.explanation: Mark 5:3-5 — "Who had his dwelling among the tombs; and no man could bind him, no, not with chains... always, night and day, he was in the mountains, and in the tombs, crying, and cutting himself with stones."

adults.question: How many swine perished when the demons entered them?
adults.option_a: About two thousand
adults.option_b: About five hundred
adults.option_c: About one hundred
adults.option_d: About ten thousand
adults.explanation: Mark 5:13 — "And the unclean spirits went out, and entered into the swine: and the herd ran violently down a steep place into the sea, (they were about two thousand;) and were choked in the sea."

adults.question: What did Jesus perceive when the woman with the issue of blood touched Him?
adults.option_a: That virtue (power) had gone out of Him
adults.option_b: That someone had stolen from Him
adults.option_c: That the crowd was too large
adults.option_d: That a sinner was nearby
adults.explanation: Mark 5:30 — "And Jesus, immediately knowing in himself that virtue had gone out of him, turned him about in the press, and said, Who touched my clothes?"

adults.question: How long had the woman suffered with her issue of blood?
adults.option_a: Twelve years
adults.option_b: Seven years
adults.option_c: Three years
adults.option_d: Twenty years
adults.explanation: Mark 5:25 — "And a certain woman, which had an issue of blood twelve years."

adults.question: What did Jesus say to the woman that identified the true cause of her healing?
adults.option_a: Thy faith hath made thee whole; go in peace, and be whole of thy plague
adults.option_b: My power has healed you
adults.option_c: Your touch has magical properties
adults.option_d: The garment itself healed you
adults.explanation: Mark 5:34 — "Daughter, thy faith hath made thee whole; go in peace, and be whole of thy plague."

adults.question: What was the response of the mourners when Jesus said the girl was not dead but sleeping?
adults.option_a: They laughed Him to scorn
adults.option_b: They believed Him immediately
adults.option_c: They fell on their knees
adults.option_d: They ran away in fear
adults.explanation: Mark 5:40 — "And they laughed him to scorn. But when he had put them all out, he taketh the father and the mother of the damsel, and them that were with him, and entereth in where the damsel was lying."

adults.question: Who did Jesus allow to witness the raising of Jairus' daughter?
adults.option_a: Peter, James, and John, along with the father and mother
adults.option_b: All twelve disciples
adults.option_c: The entire crowd
adults.option_d: Only the mother
adults.explanation: Mark 5:37,40 — "He suffered no man to follow him, save Peter, and James, and John the brother of James... he taketh the father and the mother of the damsel, and them that were with him."

adults.question: What does the juxtaposition of the woman healed after 12 years of suffering and the 12-year-old girl raised from the dead suggest theologically?
adults.option_a: Both accounts demonstrate that Jesus has absolute authority over chronic disease and death, with faith as the common thread connecting both miracles
adults.option_b: The number 12 is merely a coincidence
adults.option_c: Only women and girls can receive miracles
adults.option_d: Physical touch is required for all healings
adults.explanation: Mark 5:25,42 — The woman suffered 12 years; the girl was 12 years old. Both are restored by Jesus' power, responding to faith (Jairus' in v. 36, the woman's in v. 34).

kids.question: Why couldn't Jesus do many mighty works in His hometown?
kids.explanation: Mark 6:5-6 — "And he could there do no mighty work... And he marvelled because of their unbelief."

kids.question: How many people did Jesus feed with five loaves and two fishes?
kids.explanation: Mark 6:44 — "And they that did eat of the loaves were about five thousand men."

kids.question: What amazing thing did Jesus do on the sea?
kids.explanation: Mark 6:48 — "And he saw them toiling in rowing... and about the fourth watch of the night he cometh unto them, walking upon the sea."

teens.question: What did Jesus tell the twelve to take with them when He sent them out?
teens.option_a: Nothing except a staff — no bread, no bag, no money, but to wear sandals
teens.explanation: Mark 6:8-9 — "And commanded them that they should take nothing for their journey, save a staff only; no scrip, no bread, no money in their purse: But be shod with sandals."

teens.question: Who had John the Baptist beheaded?
teens.option_a: Herod, at the request of Herodias' daughter
teens.explanation: Mark 6:27 — "And immediately the king sent an executioner, and commanded his head to be brought."

teens.question: Why did Herod fear John the Baptist?
teens.option_a: He knew John was a just and holy man
teens.option_b: He thought John had a large army
teens.option_c: He believed John was a Roman spy
teens.option_d: He was afraid of John's magical powers
teens.explanation: Mark 6:20 — "For Herod feared John, knowing that he was a just man and an holy."

teens.question: How many baskets of leftovers were collected after the feeding of the five thousand?
teens.option_a: Twelve baskets full
teens.explanation: Mark 6:43 — "And they took up twelve baskets full of the fragments, and of the fishes."

teens.question: What did the disciples think when they saw Jesus walking on the water?
teens.option_a: They supposed it was a spirit and cried out in fear
teens.option_c: They thought it was a fisherman
teens.option_d: They ignored it and kept rowing
teens.explanation: Mark 6:49 — "But when they saw him walking upon the sea, they supposed it had been a spirit, and cried out."

adults.question: What did the people of Nazareth call Jesus, and why did they take offense?
adults.option_a: The carpenter and son of Mary — they were offended because He was a local tradesman they thought they knew
adults.option_b: The prophet from Galilee — they expected miracles
adults.option_c: The teacher from Jerusalem — they questioned His credentials
adults.option_d: The healer from Bethsaida — they doubted His origins
adults.explanation: Mark 6:3 — "Is not this the carpenter, the son of Mary... And they were offended at him."

adults.question: What proverbial saying did Jesus use about His rejection in Nazareth?
adults.option_a: A prophet is not without honour, but in his own country, and among his own kin, and in his own house
adults.option_b: No man can serve two masters
adults.option_c: The last shall be first
adults.option_d: Where your treasure is, there will your heart be
adults.explanation: Mark 6:4 — "A prophet is not without honour, but in his own country, and among his own kin, and in his own house."

adults.question: What did Herod think about Jesus after hearing of His fame?
adults.option_a: That John the Baptist was risen from the dead
adults.option_b: That a new prophet had appeared
adults.option_c: That Moses had returned
adults.option_d: That Elijah had come back
adults.explanation: Mark 6:14,16 — "And king Herod heard of him... But when Herod heard thereof, he said, It is John, whom I beheaded: he is risen from the dead."

adults.question: Why did Herodias want John the Baptist killed?
adults.option_a: Because John told Herod it was not lawful to have his brother's wife
adults.option_b: Because John refused to bow to Herod
adults.option_c: Because John predicted Herod's downfall
adults.option_d: Because John threatened to expose tax fraud
adults.explanation: Mark 6:18-19 — "For John had said unto Herod, It is not lawful for thee to have thy brother's wife. Therefore Herodias had a quarrel against him, and would have killed him."

adults.question: What emotion did Jesus feel when He saw the great multitude before the feeding miracle?
adults.option_a: Compassion, because they were as sheep not having a shepherd
adults.option_b: Frustration because they followed Him
adults.option_c: Anger at their spiritual ignorance
adults.option_d: Indifference to their physical needs
adults.explanation: Mark 6:34 — "And Jesus, when he came out, saw much people, and was moved with compassion toward them, because they were as sheep not having a shepherd."

adults.question: What does Jesus' command to the disciples — "Give ye them to eat" — reveal about His teaching method?
adults.option_a: He tested their faith and resourcefulness before demonstrating divine provision, inviting them to participate in the miracle
adults.option_b: He expected them to buy food from a nearby town
adults.option_c: He was testing their knowledge of Scripture
adults.option_d: He was being sarcastic
adults.explanation: Mark 6:37 — "Give ye them to eat." Jesus involved the disciples in the miracle, using what they had (5 loaves, 2 fish) to display divine abundance.

adults.question: What does Mark 6:48 say Jesus intended to do when walking on the sea?
adults.option_a: He would have passed by them — an allusion to Old Testament theophanies (cf. Exodus 33:19, 1 Kings 19:11)
adults.option_b: He intended to walk past them to the other shore
adults.option_c: He was testing whether they would call out
adults.option_d: He did not see them in the darkness
adults.explanation: Mark 6:48 — "And he saw them toiling in rowing... and about the fourth watch of the night he cometh unto them, walking upon the sea, and would have passed by them." This echoes God's self-revelation in the OT.

adults.question: What does Mark say was the reason the disciples were amazed at the walking on water and their hearts were hardened?
adults.option_a: They considered not the miracle of the loaves — they failed to understand what the feeding miracle revealed about Jesus' identity
adults.option_b: They were naturally afraid of the dark
adults.option_c: They had never seen the sea before
adults.option_d: They were exhausted from rowing
adults.explanation: Mark 6:52 — "For they considered not the miracle of the loaves: for their heart was hardened."

kids.question: What did Jesus say makes a person unclean?
kids.option_a: Evil things that come out from within a person's heart
kids.explanation: Mark 7:20-21 — "That which cometh out of the man, that defileth the man. For from within, out of the heart of men, proceed evil thoughts."

kids.question: What did the woman from Syrophoenicia ask Jesus to do?
kids.option_a: Cast the devil out of her daughter
kids.explanation: Mark 7:26 — "The woman was a Greek, a Syrophenician by nation; and she besought him that he would cast forth the devil out of her daughter."

kids.question: What did Jesus use to heal the deaf man?
kids.option_a: He put His fingers in the man's ears and touched his tongue
kids.option_c: He told him to wash in a river
kids.option_d: He placed a cloth on his head
kids.explanation: Mark 7:33 — "And he took him aside from the multitude, and put his fingers into his ears, and he spit, and touched his tongue."

teens.question: What did the Pharisees and scribes criticize the disciples for?
teens.option_a: Eating bread with unwashed (defiled) hands, not following the tradition of the elders
teens.explanation: Mark 7:2,5 — "They saw some of his disciples eat bread with defiled, that is to say, with unwashen, hands... Why walk not thy disciples according to the tradition of the elders?"

teens.question: What Old Testament prophet did Jesus quote against the Pharisees?
teens.option_a: Isaiah — "This people honoureth me with their lips, but their heart is far from me"
teens.option_b: Jeremiah — "The heart is deceitful above all things"
teens.option_c: Ezekiel — "I will give them a new heart"
teens.option_d: Amos — "Let justice roll down like waters"
teens.explanation: Mark 7:6 — "Well hath Esaias prophesied of you hypocrites, as it is written, This people honoureth me with their lips, but their heart is far from me."

teens.question: What practice of the Pharisees did Jesus condemn as "Corban"?
teens.option_a: Declaring money as a gift devoted to God to avoid supporting their parents
teens.option_b: Giving extra offerings to the temple
teens.option_c: Fasting twice a week for God
teens.option_d: Teaching children the Law of Moses
teens.explanation: Mark 7:11-12 — "If a man shall say to his father or mother, It is Corban, that is to say, a gift, by whatsoever thou mightest be profited by me... ye suffer him no more to do ought for his father or his mother."

teens.question: How did the Syrophoenician woman respond to Jesus' statement about the children's bread?
teens.option_a: Yes, Lord: yet the dogs under the table eat of the children's crumbs
teens.option_d: She offered to pay for the healing
teens.explanation: Mark 7:28 — "And she answered and said unto him, Yes, Lord: yet the dogs under the table eat of the children's crumbs."

teens.question: What word did Jesus say when healing the deaf man, and what does it mean?
teens.option_a: Ephphatha, which means Be opened
teens.explanation: Mark 7:34 — "And looking up to heaven, he sighed, and saith unto him, Ephphatha, that is, Be opened."

adults.question: What does Jesus' teaching in verses 18-19 declare about all foods?
adults.option_a: That nothing from outside entering a man can defile him — Mark notes this purges (cleanses) all meats
adults.option_b: That only kosher foods are acceptable
adults.option_c: That fasting is required before eating
adults.option_d: That vegetarianism is the Christian ideal
adults.explanation: Mark 7:18-19 — "Whatsoever thing from without entereth into the man, it cannot defile him... purging all meats."

adults.question: What thirteen evil things does Jesus list as coming from within the heart of man?
adults.option_a: Evil thoughts, adulteries, fornications, murders, thefts, covetousness, wickedness, deceit, lasciviousness, an evil eye, blasphemy, pride, foolishness
adults.option_b: Only hatred and jealousy
adults.option_c: Poverty, illness, and bad luck
adults.option_d: Ignorance, laziness, and poor hygiene
adults.explanation: Mark 7:21-22 — "For from within, out of the heart of men, proceed evil thoughts, adulteries, fornications, murders, Thefts, covetousness, wickedness, deceit, lasciviousness, an evil eye, blasphemy, pride, foolishness."

adults.question: How does Jesus' accusation in verse 8 distinguish between divine law and human tradition?
adults.option_a: He says the Pharisees lay aside the commandment of God to hold the tradition of men
adults.option_b: He says human tradition is more important
adults.option_c: He says both are equally authoritative
adults.option_d: He abolishes all law entirely
adults.explanation: Mark 7:8 — "For laying aside the commandment of God, ye hold the tradition of men."

adults.question: What does the "Corban" controversy in verses 10-13 reveal about the danger of religious tradition?
adults.option_a: Human traditions, even religious ones, can be used to circumvent God's moral commands — here, the fifth commandment to honour parents
adults.option_b: That all religious traditions are inherently evil
adults.option_c: That the Pharisees had good intentions
adults.option_d: That financial offerings replace moral duties
adults.explanation: Mark 7:10-13 — Jesus shows that "Corban" vows were used to evade the commandment "Honour thy father and thy mother," making God's word of no effect through tradition.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of Jesus healing the Syrophoenician woman's daughter?
adults.option_a: It demonstrates that God's grace extends beyond Israel to the Gentiles — the gospel is for all nations
adults.option_b: That Gentiles must earn their healing
adults.option_c: That Jesus only helped non-Jews reluctantly
adults.option_d: That Jewish priority in salvation is permanent
adults.explanation: Mark 7:29-30 — "For this saying go thy way; the devil is gone out of thy daughter." Jesus healed a Gentile's child, foreshadowing the gospel going to all nations.

adults.question: In what region did Jesus heal the deaf man with a speech impediment?
adults.option_a: Decapolis — a Gentile region of ten cities
adults.option_b: Jerusalem — the holy city
adults.option_c: Nazareth — His hometown
adults.option_d: Bethlehem — the city of David
adults.explanation: Mark 7:31 — "And again, departing from the coasts of Tyre and Sidon, he came unto the sea of Galilee, through the midst of the coasts of Decapolis."

adults.question: What is notable about Jesus' method of healing the deaf man (fingers in ears, spitting, touching tongue)?
adults.option_a: Jesus used physical actions to communicate with a man who could not hear verbal commands, accommodating his disability
adults.option_b: Jesus needed physical contact for all healings
adults.option_c: Saliva was considered medicine in the first century
adults.option_d: It was a ritual required by Mosaic law
adults.explanation: Mark 7:33-35 — Jesus adapted His approach to reach a man who could not hear. The physical touch communicated what words could not.

adults.question: What did the people say after the healing of the deaf man, echoing Genesis and Isaiah?
adults.option_a: He hath done all things well: he maketh both the deaf to hear, and the dumb to speak
adults.option_b: He is merely a good teacher
adults.option_c: He has power from Beelzebub
adults.option_d: He is the reincarnation of Elijah
adults.explanation: Mark 7:37 — "He hath done all things well: he maketh both the deaf to hear, and the dumb to speak." This echoes Genesis 1:31 ("very good") and Isaiah 35:5-6.

kids.question: How many loaves did Jesus use to feed four thousand people?
kids.explanation: Mark 8:5-6 — "And he asked them, How many loaves have ye? And they said, Seven... and he took the seven loaves."

kids.question: What did Peter say when Jesus asked who the disciples thought He was?
kids.explanation: Mark 8:29 — "And Peter answereth and saith unto him, Thou art the Christ."

kids.question: What did Jesus say a person must do to follow Him?
kids.option_a: Deny himself, take up his cross, and follow Me
kids.explanation: Mark 8:34 — "Whosoever will come after me, let him deny himself, and take up his cross, and follow me."

teens.question: How many baskets of leftovers were collected after the feeding of the four thousand?
teens.option_a: Seven baskets
teens.explanation: Mark 8:8 — "So they did eat, and were filled: and they took up of the broken meat that was left seven baskets."

teens.question: What did the Pharisees demand from Jesus that He refused to give?
teens.option_a: A sign from heaven
teens.explanation: Mark 8:11-12 — "And the Pharisees came forth, and began to question with him, seeking of him a sign from heaven... There shall no sign be given unto this generation."

teens.question: What did Jesus warn the disciples to beware of?
teens.option_a: The leaven of the Pharisees and the leaven of Herod
teens.option_d: The wild animals in the desert
teens.explanation: Mark 8:15 — "And he charged them, saying, Take heed, beware of the leaven of the Pharisees, and of the leaven of Herod."

teens.question: What did the blind man at Bethsaida see at first after Jesus touched him?
teens.option_a: Men as trees, walking — his sight was only partially restored at first
teens.explanation: Mark 8:24 — "And he looked up, and said, I see men as trees, walking."

teens.question: What did Jesus say would happen to someone who is ashamed of Him?
teens.option_a: The Son of man shall be ashamed of him when He comes in the glory of His Father with the holy angels
teens.option_d: They will receive a second chance
teens.explanation: Mark 8:38 — "Whosoever therefore shall be ashamed of me and of my words... of him also shall the Son of man be ashamed, when he cometh in the glory of his Father with the holy angels."

adults.question: What is the significance of Jesus' two feeding miracles — five thousand (ch. 6) and four thousand (ch. 8)?
adults.option_a: The 5,000 with 12 baskets left symbolizes ministry to Israel (12 tribes); the 4,000 with 7 baskets symbolizes ministry to Gentiles (7 representing completeness)
adults.option_b: They are the same event told twice
adults.option_c: Jesus only fed Jews in both cases
adults.option_d: The numbers are symbolic of zodiac signs
adults.explanation: Mark 8:19-20 — Jesus specifically asks the disciples to recall both miracles and the different numbers of baskets (12 and 7), suggesting distinct theological significance.

adults.question: What did Jesus mean by "the leaven of the Pharisees and Herod"?
adults.option_a: Their hypocritical teaching and corrupt influence that spreads like yeast through dough
adults.option_b: Literal bread and yeast
adults.option_c: Their baking traditions
adults.option_d: Their dietary restrictions
adults.explanation: Mark 8:15 — "Beware of the leaven of the Pharisees, and of the leaven of Herod." Leaven symbolizes corrupting influence; the Pharisees' legalism and Herod's worldliness both distort truth.

adults.question: Why is the two-stage healing of the blind man at Bethsaida unique among Jesus' miracles?
adults.option_a: It is the only recorded miracle where Jesus healed in two stages, likely illustrating the disciples' own partial understanding that needed further enlightenment
adults.option_b: Jesus' power was weakened that day
adults.option_c: The man's blindness was too severe for one touch
adults.option_d: It was a failed first attempt
adults.explanation: Mark 8:23-25 — The progressive healing mirrors the disciples' own journey from spiritual blindness to partial sight (Peter's confession) to full understanding (after the resurrection).

adults.question: What did people say about who Jesus was when He asked the disciples?
adults.option_a: John the Baptist, Elias (Elijah), or one of the prophets
adults.option_b: The Son of David, a king, or the Messiah
adults.option_c: Moses, Abraham, or Solomon
adults.option_d: An angel, a priest, or a scribe
adults.explanation: Mark 8:28 — "And they answered, John the Baptist: but some say, Elias; and others, One of the prophets."

adults.question: What did Jesus begin to teach the disciples after Peter's confession?
adults.option_a: That the Son of man must suffer many things, be rejected, be killed, and after three days rise again
adults.option_b: That they should go to Rome and preach
adults.option_c: That the kingdom would come immediately
adults.option_d: That they should establish a political movement
adults.explanation: Mark 8:31 — "And he began to teach them, that the Son of man must suffer many things, and be rejected of the elders, and of the chief priests, and scribes, and be killed, and after three days rise again."

adults.question: How did Jesus respond when Peter rebuked Him for predicting His suffering?
adults.option_a: Get thee behind me, Satan: for thou savourest not the things that be of God, but the things that be of men
adults.option_b: Thank you, Peter, for your concern
adults.option_c: I will reconsider my path
adults.option_d: You may be right about this
adults.explanation: Mark 8:33 — "But when he had turned about and looked on his disciples, he rebuked Peter, saying, Get thee behind me, Satan: for thou savourest not the things that be of God, but the things that be of men."

adults.question: What rhetorical question does Jesus ask about the value of a soul?
adults.option_a: What shall it profit a man, if he shall gain the whole world, and lose his own soul?
adults.option_b: How much gold is a man's life worth?
adults.option_c: What shall a man give in exchange for his freedom?
adults.option_d: Who can measure the value of wisdom?
adults.explanation: Mark 8:36 — "For what shall it profit a man, if he shall gain the whole world, and lose his own soul?"

adults.question: How does Peter's confession in Mark 8:29 serve as the structural pivot of Mark's Gospel?
adults.option_a: It marks the transition from the first half (who is Jesus? — revealed through miracles) to the second half (what must Jesus do? — the way of the cross)
adults.option_b: It is merely one of many confessions in the book
adults.option_c: It represents the end of Jesus' ministry
adults.option_d: It has no special literary significance
adults.explanation: Mark 8:29 — "Thou art the Christ." This confession divides Mark: chapters 1-8 reveal Jesus' identity; chapters 9-16 reveal His mission of suffering, death, and resurrection.

kids.question: What happened to Jesus' clothes on the mountain?
kids.option_a: They became shining, exceeding white as snow
kids.explanation: Mark 9:3 — "And his raiment became shining, exceeding white as snow; so as no fuller on earth can white them."

kids.question: Who appeared with Jesus on the mountain?
kids.explanation: Mark 9:4 — "And there appeared unto them Elias with Moses: and they were talking with Jesus."

kids.question: What did Jesus say about being the greatest?
kids.option_a: If any man desire to be first, the same shall be last of all and servant of all
kids.option_b: The strongest person is the greatest
kids.option_c: The richest person is the greatest
kids.option_d: The oldest person is the greatest
kids.explanation: Mark 9:35 — "If any man desire to be first, the same shall be last of all, and servant of all."

teens.question: What did the voice from the cloud say at the Transfiguration?
teens.option_a: This is my beloved Son: hear him
teens.option_c: Worship the Lord thy God only
teens.option_d: Fear not, I am with you
teens.explanation: Mark 9:7 — "And there was a cloud that overshadowed them: and a voice came out of the cloud, saying, This is my beloved Son: hear him."

teens.question: What did the father of the demon-possessed boy cry out to Jesus?
teens.option_a: Lord, I believe; help thou mine unbelief
teens.option_b: I have no faith at all
teens.option_c: I only believe in miracles I can see
teens.explanation: Mark 9:24 — "And straightway the father of the child cried out, and said with tears, Lord, I believe; help thou mine unbelief."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about the kind of spirit that possessed the boy?
teens.option_a: This kind can come forth by nothing, but by prayer and fasting
teens.option_b: Any disciple can easily cast this out
teens.option_d: This kind only leaves at sunset
teens.explanation: Mark 9:29 — "And he said unto them, This kind can come forth by nothing, but by prayer and fasting."

teens.question: What did Jesus do when the disciples argued about who was the greatest?
teens.option_a: He set a little child in the midst of them and said receiving a child in His name is receiving Him
teens.option_b: He chose Peter as the greatest
teens.option_c: He told them to stop talking
teens.explanation: Mark 9:36-37 — "And he took a child, and set him in the midst of them: and when he had taken him in his arms, he said unto them, Whosoever shall receive one of such children in my name, receiveth me."

teens.question: What vivid warning did Jesus give about causing a little one to stumble?
teens.option_a: It is better for him that a millstone were hanged about his neck, and he were cast into the sea
teens.option_b: He should pay a fine to the temple
teens.option_c: He should fast for a week
teens.option_d: He should move to another city
teens.explanation: Mark 9:42 — "And whosoever shall offend one of these little ones that believe in me, it is better for him that a millstone were hanged about his neck, and he were cast into the sea."

adults.question: Which three disciples witnessed the Transfiguration?
adults.option_a: Peter, James, and John
adults.option_b: Peter, Andrew, and Matthew
adults.option_c: James, John, and Thomas
adults.option_d: Peter, Philip, and Bartholomew
adults.explanation: Mark 9:2 — "And after six days Jesus taketh with him Peter, and James, and John, and leadeth them up into an high mountain apart by themselves."

adults.question: What did Peter suggest building on the mount of Transfiguration?
adults.option_a: Three tabernacles — one for Jesus, one for Moses, and one for Elijah
adults.option_b: A permanent temple
adults.option_c: An altar of sacrifice
adults.option_d: A stone monument
adults.explanation: Mark 9:5 — "Master, it is good for us to be here: and let us make three tabernacles; one for thee, and one for Moses, and one for Elias."

adults.question: What did Jesus charge the three disciples after the Transfiguration?
adults.option_a: To tell no man what they had seen until the Son of man were risen from the dead
adults.option_b: To immediately tell everyone
adults.option_c: To write it down in a book
adults.option_d: To tell only the other nine disciples
adults.explanation: Mark 9:9 — "And as they came down from the mountain, he charged them that they should tell no man what things they had seen, till the Son of man were risen from the dead."

adults.question: What did Jesus say about Elias (Elijah) having already come?
adults.option_a: That Elias has indeed come, and they have done unto him whatsoever they listed — referring to John the Baptist
adults.option_b: That Elijah was still expected in the future
adults.option_c: That the prophecy about Elijah was symbolic only
adults.option_d: That Elijah appeared at the Transfiguration as fulfillment
adults.explanation: Mark 9:13 — "But I say unto you, That Elias is indeed come, and they have done unto him whatsoever they listed, as it is written of him."

adults.question: What does Jesus' statement "All things are possible to him that believeth" teach about faith?
adults.option_a: That genuine faith connects a person to the limitless power of God, making the impossible possible through Him
adults.option_b: That humans have unlimited natural power
adults.option_c: That faith is merely positive thinking
adults.option_d: That believing guarantees material prosperity
adults.explanation: Mark 9:23 — "Jesus said unto him, If thou canst believe, all things are possible to him that believeth."

adults.question: What repeated phrase does Jesus use about hell (Gehenna) in verses 43-48?
adults.option_a: Where their worm dieth not, and the fire is not quenched
adults.option_b: Where there is weeping and gnashing of teeth
adults.option_c: Where the darkness is eternal
adults.option_d: Where the soul sleeps forever
adults.explanation: Mark 9:44,46,48 — "Where their worm dieth not, and the fire is not quenched." Jesus quotes Isaiah 66:24 three times for emphasis.

adults.question: What does Jesus mean by "Have salt in yourselves, and have peace one with another" in verse 50?
adults.option_a: Believers should maintain their distinctive, preserving influence (salt) and live in harmony with each other
adults.option_b: Literal salt should be added to all meals
adults.option_c: Salt is a symbol of wealth and prosperity
adults.option_d: Only peacemakers can enter the kingdom
adults.explanation: Mark 9:50 — "Salt is good: but if the salt have lost his saltness, wherewith will ye season it? Have salt in yourselves, and have peace one with another."

adults.question: How does the Transfiguration confirm Jesus' identity and mission?
adults.option_a: It reveals His pre-incarnate divine glory, connects Him to the Law (Moses) and Prophets (Elijah), and the Father's voice affirms His unique Sonship
adults.option_b: It was merely a vision with no theological meaning
adults.option_c: It shows Jesus was equal to Moses and Elijah
adults.option_d: It proves the disciples had spiritual powers
adults.explanation: Mark 9:2-7 — The Transfiguration unveils Christ's glory (v. 3), connects Him to the OT witness (v. 4), and the Father commands "Hear him" (v. 7), superseding Moses and Elijah.

kids.question: What did Jesus say when the disciples tried to keep the children away?
kids.option_a: Suffer the little children to come unto me, and forbid them not
kids.option_b: Children should wait until they are older
kids.option_c: Only adults can come to me
kids.option_d: Tell the children to go home
kids.explanation: Mark 10:14 — "Suffer the little children to come unto me, and forbid them not: for of such is the kingdom of God."

kids.question: What did the rich young man have a lot of?
kids.explanation: Mark 10:22 — "And he was sad at that saying, and went away grieved: for he had great possessions."

kids.question: Who did Jesus heal on the road near Jericho?
kids.explanation: Mark 10:46,52 — "Blind Bartimaeus, the son of Timaeus, sat by the highway side begging... And Jesus said unto him, Go thy way; thy faith hath made thee whole."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about divorce when the Pharisees tested Him?
teens.option_a: What God hath joined together, let not man put asunder
teens.option_b: Divorce is acceptable for any reason
teens.option_d: Moses' law is the final word
teens.explanation: Mark 10:9 — "What therefore God hath joined together, let not man put asunder."

teens.question: What did Jesus say is hard for rich people to do?
teens.option_a: Enter into the kingdom of God
teens.explanation: Mark 10:23 — "How hardly shall they that have riches enter into the kingdom of God!"

teens.question: What famous comparison did Jesus make about a rich man entering the kingdom?
teens.option_a: It is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich man to enter the kingdom of God
teens.option_b: It is easier for a horse to fly
teens.option_c: It is easier for a mountain to move
teens.option_d: It is easier for the sun to stop
teens.explanation: Mark 10:25 — "It is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle, than for a rich man to enter into the kingdom of God."

teens.question: What did James and John ask Jesus for?
teens.option_a: To sit on His right and left hand in His glory
teens.option_b: To be released from following Him
teens.option_d: To lead the other ten disciples
teens.explanation: Mark 10:37 — "They said unto him, Grant unto us that we may sit, one on thy right hand, and the other on thy left hand, in thy glory."

teens.question: What did blind Bartimaeus cry out to Jesus?
teens.option_a: Jesus, thou Son of David, have mercy on me
teens.option_c: Jesus, take me as your disciple
teens.explanation: Mark 10:47 — "And when he heard that it was Jesus of Nazareth, he began to cry out, and say, Jesus, thou Son of David, have mercy on me."

adults.question: Why did Jesus say Moses permitted a bill of divorcement?
adults.option_a: For the hardness of your hearts he wrote you this precept
adults.option_b: Because marriage is temporary
adults.option_c: Because divorce is God's perfect will
adults.option_d: Because Moses disliked marriage
adults.explanation: Mark 10:5 — "And Jesus answered and said unto them, For the hardness of your heart he wrote you this precept."

adults.question: What does Jesus say about receiving the kingdom of God like a child?
adults.option_a: Whosoever shall not receive the kingdom of God as a little child, he shall not enter therein
adults.option_b: Only literal children can enter the kingdom
adults.option_c: Adults should act immature
adults.option_d: Children are automatically saved
adults.explanation: Mark 10:15 — "Verily I say unto you, Whosoever shall not receive the kingdom of God as a little child, he shall not enter therein."

adults.question: What did the rich young ruler claim about keeping the commandments?
adults.option_a: Master, all these have I observed from my youth
adults.option_b: He admitted he had broken them all
adults.option_c: He said he knew only a few
adults.option_d: He asked which commandments Jesus meant
adults.explanation: Mark 10:20 — "And he answered and said unto him, Master, all these have I observed from my youth."

adults.question: How did Jesus feel when He looked at the rich young ruler?
adults.option_a: He loved him — then told him to sell all he had, give to the poor, and follow Him
adults.option_b: He was angry with him
adults.option_c: He was indifferent
adults.option_d: He was suspicious of his motives
adults.explanation: Mark 10:21 — "Then Jesus beholding him loved him, and said unto him, One thing thou lackest: go thy way, sell whatsoever thou hast, and give to the poor."

adults.question: What promise does Jesus make about those who leave everything for Him and the gospel?
adults.option_a: They shall receive an hundredfold now in this time, and in the world to come eternal life
adults.option_b: They will become wealthy immediately
adults.option_c: They will suffer without reward
adults.option_d: They will be forgotten by God
adults.explanation: Mark 10:29-30 — "There is no man that hath left house, or brethren... but he shall receive an hundredfold now in this time... and in the world to come eternal life."

adults.question: What does Jesus say the Son of man came to do?
adults.option_a: Not to be ministered unto, but to minister, and to give his life a ransom for many
adults.option_b: To be served by all nations
adults.option_c: To establish an earthly kingdom
adults.option_d: To overthrow the Roman Empire
adults.explanation: Mark 10:45 — "For even the Son of man came not to be ministered unto, but to minister, and to give his life a ransom for many."

adults.question: What "cup" and "baptism" does Jesus ask James and John about?
adults.option_a: His suffering and death — the cup of God's wrath and the baptism of His crucifixion
adults.option_b: The communion cup and water baptism
adults.option_c: A cup of wine and ritual washing
adults.option_d: The cup of blessing and Spirit baptism
adults.explanation: Mark 10:38 — "Can ye drink of the cup that I drink of? and be baptized with the baptism that I am baptized with?" The cup and baptism refer to His atoning death.

adults.question: How does Mark 10:45 serve as a key verse summarizing Mark's entire Gospel?
adults.option_a: It encapsulates Jesus as the suffering Servant who came to serve and give His life as a substitutionary ransom — the central theme of Mark
adults.option_b: It is a minor teaching among many
adults.option_c: It only applies to the twelve disciples
adults.option_d: It refers to a future, not present, mission
adults.explanation: Mark 10:45 — "To give his life a ransom for many." This verse is often called the key verse of Mark, summarizing Jesus' mission as the Servant-Redeemer (cf. Isaiah 53).

kids.question: What animal did Jesus ride into Jerusalem?
kids.explanation: Mark 11:7 — "And they brought the colt to Jesus, and cast their garments on him; and he sat upon him."

kids.question: What did the people shout as Jesus entered Jerusalem?
kids.option_a: Hosanna; Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord
kids.option_d: Long live the king of Rome
kids.explanation: Mark 11:9 — "Hosanna; Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord."

kids.question: What did Jesus do to the money changers in the temple?
kids.option_a: He overturned their tables and drove them out
kids.option_c: He sat down and talked with them
kids.explanation: Mark 11:15 — "And Jesus went into the temple, and began to cast out them that sold and bought in the temple, and overthrew the tables of the moneychangers."

teens.question: What did Jesus find on the fig tree when He was hungry?
teens.option_a: Nothing but leaves — it had no fruit
teens.explanation: Mark 11:13 — "And seeing a fig tree afar off having leaves, he came, if haply he might find any thing thereon: and when he came to it, he found nothing but leaves."

teens.question: What did Jesus say the temple should be called?
teens.option_a: A house of prayer for all nations
teens.explanation: Mark 11:17 — "Is it not written, My house shall be called of all nations the house of prayer? but ye have made it a den of thieves."

teens.question: What happened to the fig tree the morning after Jesus cursed it?
teens.option_a: It was dried up from the roots
teens.explanation: Mark 11:20 — "And in the morning, as they passed by, they saw the fig tree dried up from the roots."

teens.question: What did Jesus teach about prayer using the fig tree as an example?
teens.option_a: Whatever you ask in prayer, believe that you receive it, and you shall have it
teens.option_b: Prayer only works in the temple
teens.option_c: Only priests can have effective prayers
teens.explanation: Mark 11:24 — "What things soever ye desire, when ye pray, believe that ye receive them, and ye shall have them."

teens.question: What condition did Jesus add about forgiveness and prayer?
teens.option_a: When ye stand praying, forgive, if ye have ought against any, that your Father may forgive you
teens.option_b: Forgiveness is not connected to prayer
teens.explanation: Mark 11:25 — "And when ye stand praying, forgive, if ye have ought against any: that your Father also which is in heaven may forgive you your trespasses."

adults.question: What Old Testament prophecy did Jesus fulfill by riding into Jerusalem on a colt?
adults.option_a: Zechariah 9:9 — "Thy King cometh unto thee... lowly, and riding upon an ass, and upon a colt the foal of an ass"
adults.option_b: Isaiah 53 — the suffering servant
adults.option_c: Malachi 3:1 — the messenger
adults.option_d: Daniel 7:13 — the Son of man coming in clouds
adults.explanation: Mark 11:7,9 — Jesus' entry on a colt fulfills Zechariah 9:9, presenting Himself as the humble, peaceful Messianic King.

adults.question: What does the cursing of the fig tree symbolize?
adults.option_a: God's judgment on Israel for having the appearance of spiritual life (leaves) but bearing no fruit of genuine faith
adults.option_b: That fig trees are inferior to other trees
adults.option_c: That Jesus was unreasonably angry
adults.option_d: That agriculture was failing in Palestine
adults.explanation: Mark 11:13-14,20 — The fig tree with leaves but no fruit symbolizes Israel's religious show without genuine righteousness. Its withering foreshadows judgment on the temple.

adults.question: How does the temple cleansing narrative bracket the fig tree episode (Mark 11:12-21)?
adults.option_a: Mark uses a "sandwich" literary structure: fig tree cursed (A), temple cleansed (B), fig tree withered (A') — linking fruitlessness to temple corruption
adults.option_b: The events are completely unrelated
adults.option_c: The fig tree episode was an afterthought
adults.option_d: Mark simply recorded events chronologically
adults.explanation: Mark 11:12-21 — Mark's intercalation (sandwich) technique links the barren fig tree to the corrupt temple, both judged for outward show without genuine devotion.

adults.question: What Scripture quotation does Jesus combine when cleansing the temple?
adults.option_a: Isaiah 56:7 ("My house shall be called of all nations the house of prayer") and Jeremiah 7:11 ("a den of thieves")
adults.option_b: Psalm 23 and Proverbs 3
adults.option_c: Genesis 1 and Exodus 20
adults.option_d: Deuteronomy 6 and Leviticus 19
adults.explanation: Mark 11:17 — Jesus merges Isaiah 56:7 with Jeremiah 7:11, condemning the exploitation of worshippers and exclusion of Gentiles from the temple courts.

adults.question: What does Jesus' saying about moving a mountain into the sea illustrate?
adults.option_a: The extraordinary power available through faith-filled prayer — nothing is impossible for God
adults.option_b: That literal mountains should be moved
adults.option_c: That prayer replaces physical action
adults.option_d: That geography is meaningless to God
adults.explanation: Mark 11:23 — "Whosoever shall say unto this mountain, Be thou removed, and be thou cast into the sea; and shall not doubt in his heart... he shall have whatsoever he saith."

adults.question: How did Jesus respond when the chief priests asked by what authority He acted?
adults.option_a: He counter-questioned them about the baptism of John — whether it was from heaven or of men
adults.option_b: He showed them a written decree
adults.option_c: He performed a miracle as proof
adults.option_d: He quoted the Law of Moses
adults.explanation: Mark 11:29-30 — "I will also ask of you one question... The baptism of John, was it from heaven, or of men?"

adults.question: Why could the chief priests not answer Jesus' question about John's baptism?
adults.option_a: If they said from heaven, Jesus would ask why they did not believe John; if from men, they feared the people
adults.option_b: They did not know who John was
adults.option_c: They had not considered the question
adults.option_d: They were forbidden to discuss baptism
adults.explanation: Mark 11:31-32 — "If we shall say, From heaven; he will say, Why then did ye not believe him? But if we shall say, Of men; they feared the people."

adults.question: What is the theological significance of "Hosanna in the highest" and "Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord"?
adults.option_a: The crowd quotes Psalm 118:25-26, a Messianic psalm used during Passover, recognizing Jesus as the long-awaited Davidic King
adults.option_b: It was a common greeting for any visitor
adults.option_c: The people were merely following Roman custom
adults.option_d: The words had no special religious meaning
adults.explanation: Mark 11:9-10 — "Hosanna; Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord: Blessed be the kingdom of our father David." Psalm 118 was a Hallel psalm with Messianic expectations.

kids.question: In Jesus' story, what did the wicked farmers do to the owner's son?
kids.explanation: Mark 12:7-8 — "This is the heir; come, let us kill him... And they took him, and killed him, and cast him out of the vineyard."

kids.question: What is the greatest commandment according to Jesus?
kids.option_a: Love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, soul, mind, and strength
kids.explanation: Mark 12:30 — "And thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy mind, and with all thy strength."

kids.question: What did the poor widow put into the temple treasury?
kids.explanation: Mark 12:42 — "And there came a certain poor widow, and she threw in two mites, which make a farthing."

teens.question: How did Jesus answer the trick question about paying taxes to Caesar?
teens.option_a: Render to Caesar the things that are Caesar's, and to God the things that are God's
teens.option_c: Pay double taxes to show obedience
teens.explanation: Mark 12:17 — "Render to Caesar the things that are Caesar's, and to God the things that are God's."

teens.question: What did the Sadducees try to trick Jesus with?
teens.option_a: A question about the resurrection involving a woman married to seven brothers
teens.option_d: A question about clean and unclean foods
teens.explanation: Mark 12:23 — "In the resurrection therefore, when they shall rise, whose wife shall she be of them? for the seven had her to wife."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about marriage in the resurrection?
teens.option_a: They neither marry nor are given in marriage but are as the angels in heaven
teens.explanation: Mark 12:25 — "For when they shall rise from the dead, they neither marry, nor are given in marriage; but are as the angels which are in heaven."

teens.question: What is the second greatest commandment?
teens.option_a: Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself
teens.explanation: Mark 12:31 — "And the second is like, namely this, Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself."

teens.question: Why did Jesus say the widow gave more than all the rich people?
teens.option_a: She gave all she had to live on, while they gave out of their abundance
teens.option_d: She gave the most beautiful coins
teens.explanation: Mark 12:44 — "For all they did cast in of their abundance; but she of her want did cast in all that she had, even all her living."

adults.question: Who does the "beloved son" represent in the parable of the wicked husbandmen?
adults.option_a: Jesus Christ — sent by God the Father (the householder) to Israel (the vineyard), and killed by the religious leaders (the husbandmen)
adults.option_b: Any generic prophet
adults.option_c: John the Baptist
adults.option_d: King David
adults.explanation: Mark 12:6-8 — "Having yet therefore one son, his wellbeloved, he sent him also last unto them, saying, They will reverence my son. But those husbandmen said... let us kill him."

adults.question: What Scripture does Jesus quote after the parable of the wicked husbandmen?
adults.option_a: The stone which the builders rejected is become the head of the corner — Psalm 118:22-23
adults.option_b: The Lord is my shepherd — Psalm 23:1
adults.option_c: In the beginning God created — Genesis 1:1
adults.option_d: The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom — Proverbs 9:10
adults.explanation: Mark 12:10-11 — "The stone which the builders rejected is become the head of the corner: This was the Lord's doing, and it is marvellous in our eyes."

adults.question: How did Jesus prove the resurrection to the Sadducees from the Torah?
adults.option_a: He quoted Exodus 3:6 — God said "I am the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob," not "I was" — He is God of the living, not the dead
adults.option_b: He quoted Daniel's vision of the resurrection
adults.option_c: He pointed to Elijah raising the dead
adults.option_d: He cited the story of Lazarus
adults.explanation: Mark 12:26-27 — "I am the God of Abraham, and the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob? He is not the God of the dead, but the God of the living."

adults.question: What did the scribe say when Jesus gave the two great commandments?
adults.option_a: That to love God and neighbor is more than all whole burnt offerings and sacrifices
adults.option_b: That Jesus was wrong
adults.option_c: That the commandments cannot be ranked
adults.option_d: That sacrifice is more important than love
adults.explanation: Mark 12:33 — "And to love him with all the heart... and to love his neighbour as himself, is more than all whole burnt offerings and sacrifices."

adults.question: What did Jesus say to the scribe who answered wisely?
adults.option_a: Thou art not far from the kingdom of God
adults.option_b: You are already in the kingdom
adults.option_c: You are very far from truth
adults.option_d: You need to fast more
adults.explanation: Mark 12:34 — "And when Jesus saw that he answered discreetly, he said unto him, Thou art not far from the kingdom of God."
