adults.question: What question did Jesus raise about David and the Messiah using Psalm 110?
adults.option_a: If David calls the Messiah "Lord," how is He then merely David's son? — pointing to the Messiah's divinity
adults.option_b: He asked why David was a shepherd
adults.option_c: He questioned David's right to be king
adults.option_d: He asked why David wrote psalms
adults.explanation: Mark 12:37 — "David therefore himself calleth him Lord; and whence is he then his son?" Jesus uses Psalm 110:1 to show the Messiah is both David's descendant and David's Lord.

adults.question: What did Jesus warn about the scribes?
adults.option_a: They love long robes, chief seats, and long prayers, but devour widows' houses — they shall receive greater damnation
adults.option_b: They are humble servants
adults.option_c: They are the most righteous people
adults.option_d: They deserve honor above all
adults.explanation: Mark 12:38-40 — "Beware of the scribes, which love to go in long clothing, and love salutations... and for a pretence make long prayers: these shall receive greater damnation."

adults.question: What principle about giving does the widow's mites episode teach?
adults.option_a: God evaluates giving not by the amount but by the sacrifice — the widow gave proportionally more because she gave everything she had
adults.option_b: That only small donations please God
adults.option_c: That the temple treasury was poorly managed
adults.option_d: That widows should not give to the temple
adults.explanation: Mark 12:43-44 — "This poor widow hath cast more in, than all they which have cast into the treasury: For all they did cast in of their abundance; but she of her want did cast in all that she had."

kids.question: What did Jesus say would happen to the temple?
kids.option_a: Not one stone would be left upon another — it would all be thrown down
kids.option_d: It would float into the sky
kids.explanation: Mark 13:2 — "Seest thou these great buildings? there shall not be left one stone upon another, that shall not be thrown down."

kids.question: What did Jesus say about false Christs?
kids.option_a: Many shall come saying I am Christ, but do not believe them
kids.option_b: No one would ever pretend to be Christ
kids.option_c: Only one false Christ would appear
kids.explanation: Mark 13:6 — "For many shall come in my name, saying, I am Christ; and shall deceive many."

kids.question: What did Jesus tell His followers to do?
kids.explanation: Mark 13:37 — "And what I say unto you I say unto all, Watch."

teens.question: What signs did Jesus give before the end?
teens.option_a: Wars, earthquakes, famines, and persecution — but the gospel must first be published among all nations
teens.explanation: Mark 13:7-10 — "And when ye shall hear of wars and rumours of wars, be ye not troubled... and the gospel must first be published among all nations."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about the "abomination of desolation"?
teens.option_a: When ye shall see it standing where it ought not, let those in Judaea flee to the mountains
teens.option_b: It refers to a beautiful new statue
teens.explanation: Mark 13:14 — "But when ye shall see the abomination of desolation, spoken of by Daniel the prophet, standing where it ought not... then let them that be in Judaea flee to the mountains."

teens.question: How will the Son of man come according to Jesus?
teens.option_a: In the clouds with great power and glory
teens.explanation: Mark 13:26 — "And then shall they see the Son of man coming in the clouds with great power and glory."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about heaven and earth?
teens.option_a: Heaven and earth shall pass away, but my words shall not pass away
teens.option_c: His words would eventually be forgotten
teens.explanation: Mark 13:31 — "Heaven and earth shall pass away: but my words shall not pass away."

teens.question: Does anyone know the day and hour of Christ's return?
teens.option_a: No, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father
teens.option_b: The prophets know the exact date
teens.option_c: It was revealed to the apostles
teens.option_d: It can be calculated from Scripture
teens.explanation: Mark 13:32 — "But of that day and that hour knoweth no man, no, not the angels which are in heaven, neither the Son, but the Father."

adults.question: Which four disciples asked Jesus privately about the destruction of the temple?
adults.option_a: Peter, James, John, and Andrew
adults.option_b: Peter, James, John, and Matthew
adults.option_c: Peter, Andrew, Philip, and Thomas
adults.option_d: James, John, Judas, and Bartholomew
adults.explanation: Mark 13:3 — "And as he sat upon the mount of Olives over against the temple, Peter and James and John and Andrew asked him privately."

adults.question: What does Jesus promise the Holy Spirit will do when believers are brought before rulers?
adults.option_a: It is not ye that speak, but the Holy Ghost — He will give them the words to say
adults.option_b: They should memorize speeches in advance
adults.option_c: They should hire lawyers to defend them
adults.option_d: They should remain silent in court
adults.explanation: Mark 13:11 — "But when they shall lead you, and deliver you up, take no thought beforehand what ye shall speak... for it is not ye that speak, but the Holy Ghost."

adults.question: What does "the abomination of desolation spoken of by Daniel the prophet" refer to historically and prophetically?
adults.option_a: It references Daniel 9:27 and 12:11 — historically fulfilled in the Roman destruction of the temple (AD 70) and potentially pointing to a future end-time desecration
adults.option_b: It refers only to Antiochus Epiphanes
adults.option_c: It was fulfilled entirely in the Babylonian exile
adults.option_d: It has no specific historical referent
adults.explanation: Mark 13:14 — "The abomination of desolation, spoken of by Daniel the prophet." This echoes Daniel 9:27 and was partially fulfilled in AD 70 when Rome destroyed the temple.

adults.question: What does Jesus say about the tribulation in verse 19?
adults.option_a: It will be the worst affliction since the creation until now, and never shall be again
adults.option_b: It will be a time of moderate difficulty
adults.option_c: It will be shorter than previous trials
adults.option_d: It will affect only Gentiles
adults.explanation: Mark 13:19 — "For in those days shall be affliction, such as was not from the beginning of the creation which God created unto this time, neither shall be."

adults.question: For whose sake did Jesus say those days would be shortened?
adults.option_a: The elect's sake, whom He hath chosen
adults.option_b: The Roman emperor's sake
adults.option_c: The high priest's sake
adults.option_d: The sake of the angels
adults.explanation: Mark 13:20 — "And except that the Lord had shortened those days, no flesh should be saved: but for the elect's sake, whom he hath chosen, he hath shortened the days."

adults.question: What cosmic signs will accompany the Son of man's coming?
adults.option_a: The sun shall be darkened, the moon shall not give her light, the stars shall fall, and the powers in heaven shall be shaken
adults.option_b: A great rainbow will appear
adults.option_c: The seas will turn to glass
adults.option_d: All mountains will crumble
adults.explanation: Mark 13:24-25 — "But in those days, after that tribulation, the sun shall be darkened, and the moon shall not give her light, And the stars of heaven shall fall, and the powers that are in heaven shall be shaken."

adults.question: What parable does Jesus use to illustrate watchfulness at the end of chapter 13?
adults.option_a: The parable of the man travelling to a far country who left his servants in charge, commanding the porter to watch
adults.option_b: The parable of the ten virgins
adults.option_c: The parable of the talents
adults.option_d: The parable of the wheat and tares
adults.explanation: Mark 13:34 — "For the Son of man is as a man taking a far journey, who left his house, and gave authority to his servants, and to every man his work, and commanded the porter to watch."

adults.question: How does Jesus' statement that "neither the Son" knows the day and hour relate to His dual nature?
adults.option_a: In His human nature, Jesus voluntarily limited His omniscience; as God the Son, all things are known to Him — this reflects the mystery of the incarnation
adults.option_b: It proves Jesus is not truly divine
adults.option_c: It means Jesus is inferior to the Father
adults.option_d: It was simply a figure of speech
adults.explanation: Mark 13:32 — "Neither the Son, but the Father." This is understood as reflecting Christ's voluntary self-limitation in His incarnate state (cf. Philippians 2:7).

kids.question: What did the woman pour on Jesus' head?
kids.explanation: Mark 14:3 — "There came a woman having an alabaster box of ointment of spikenard very precious; and she brake the box, and poured it on his head."

kids.question: What did Jesus share with His disciples at the Last Supper?
kids.option_a: Bread and wine, saying the bread was His body and the wine His blood
kids.explanation: Mark 14:22-24 — "Take, eat: this is my body... This is my blood of the new testament, which is shed for many."

kids.question: How did Judas show the soldiers which man was Jesus?
kids.explanation: Mark 14:44-45 — "Whomsoever I shall kiss, that same is he... and kissed him."

teens.question: How much was the woman's ointment worth?
teens.option_a: More than three hundred pence (nearly a year's wages)
teens.explanation: Mark 14:5 — "For it might have been sold for more than three hundred pence, and have been given to the poor."

teens.question: What did Jesus predict Peter would do?
teens.option_a: Deny Him three times before the cock crew twice
teens.explanation: Mark 14:30 — "Verily I say unto thee, That this day, even in this night, before the cock crow twice, thou shalt deny me thrice."

teens.question: What did Jesus pray in the Garden of Gethsemane?
teens.option_a: Abba, Father, all things are possible unto thee; take away this cup from me: nevertheless not what I will, but what thou wilt
teens.option_b: Let me escape from my enemies
teens.option_d: Send angels to protect me from all harm
teens.explanation: Mark 14:36 — "Abba, Father, all things are possible unto thee; take away this cup from me: nevertheless not what I will, but what thou wilt."

teens.question: What did the disciples do when Jesus was arrested?
teens.option_a: They all forsook Him and fled
teens.option_c: They followed Him to the trial
teens.explanation: Mark 14:50 — "And they all forsook him, and fled."

teens.question: What happened when Peter was accused of being with Jesus?
teens.option_a: He denied it three times, and then the cock crew and he wept
teens.explanation: Mark 14:72 — "And the second time the cock crew. And Peter called to mind the word that Jesus said unto him... And when he thought thereon, he wept."

adults.question: What did Jesus say about the woman who anointed Him?
adults.option_a: She is come aforehand to anoint my body to the burying — and wherever the gospel is preached, her deed shall be told as a memorial
adults.option_b: She wasted expensive ointment
adults.option_c: She should have given to the poor instead
adults.option_d: Her act was merely symbolic
adults.explanation: Mark 14:8-9 — "She is come aforehand to anoint my body to the burying. Verily I say unto you, Wheresoever this gospel shall be preached throughout the whole world, this also that she hath done shall be spoken of for a memorial of her."

adults.question: How much did Judas receive for betraying Jesus?
adults.option_a: The chief priests promised him money — Mark does not specify the amount, but Matthew 26:15 records thirty pieces of silver
adults.option_b: One hundred gold coins
adults.option_c: Nothing — he did it freely
adults.option_d: A piece of land
adults.explanation: Mark 14:10-11 — "And Judas Iscariot, one of the twelve, went unto the chief priests, to betray him unto them... and they promised to give him money."

adults.question: What words did Jesus use to institute the Lord's Supper?
adults.option_a: Take, eat: this is my body... This is my blood of the new testament, which is shed for many
adults.option_b: Let us eat in remembrance of Moses
adults.option_c: This food shall sustain you forever
adults.option_d: Break bread and share with strangers
adults.explanation: Mark 14:22,24 — "Take, eat: this is my body... This is my blood of the new testament, which is shed for many."

adults.question: What does Jesus' use of "Abba" in Gethsemane reveal?
adults.option_a: An intimate Aramaic term for Father, revealing Christ's unique filial relationship with God even in His deepest anguish
adults.option_b: A formal title used by all Jews
adults.option_c: A Greek philosophical concept
adults.option_d: A term meaning "teacher"
adults.explanation: Mark 14:36 — "Abba, Father." Abba is an intimate Aramaic address, showing Jesus' tender, trusting relationship with the Father even facing the cross.

adults.question: What claim did Jesus make before the high priest that was declared blasphemy?
adults.option_a: When asked if He was the Christ, the Son of the Blessed, Jesus said "I am" and quoted Daniel 7:13 about the Son of man sitting at the right hand of power
adults.option_b: He claimed to be an angel
adults.option_c: He said He was greater than Moses
adults.option_d: He declared the temple would be rebuilt in three days
adults.explanation: Mark 14:61-62 — "Art thou the Christ, the Son of the Blessed? And Jesus said, I am: and ye shall see the Son of man sitting on the right hand of power, and coming in the clouds of heaven."

adults.question: What detail unique to Mark describes a young man who fled naked at Jesus' arrest?
adults.option_a: A young man wearing only a linen cloth was seized; he left the cloth and fled naked — many scholars believe this was Mark himself
adults.option_b: A soldier lost his uniform
adults.option_c: Peter threw off his cloak
adults.option_d: An angel appeared and vanished
adults.explanation: Mark 14:51-52 — "And there followed him a certain young man, having a linen cloth cast about his naked body; and the young men laid hold on him: And he left the linen cloth, and fled from them naked."

adults.question: What does Jesus' statement "I am" (ego eimi) before the Sanhedrin echo?
adults.option_a: The divine name revealed to Moses at the burning bush (Exodus 3:14) — "I AM THAT I AM" — a direct claim to deity
adults.option_b: A common Greek greeting
adults.option_c: An acknowledgment of His human identity
adults.option_d: A phrase used by all Jewish teachers
adults.explanation: Mark 14:62 — "I am." Jesus' use of "I am" (ego eimi) in response to the question of His identity deliberately echoes the divine self-revelation of Exodus 3:14.

adults.question: How does Peter's denial fulfill Jesus' prophecy and what does the cock crowing twice signify?
adults.option_a: Jesus predicted the specific detail of two cock-crows (unique to Mark), showing His foreknowledge; Peter's failure under pressure contrasts with his earlier boast of loyalty
adults.option_b: The rooster crowing was coincidental
adults.option_c: Peter denied Jesus only once
adults.option_d: The cock crowing was a metaphor for morning
adults.explanation: Mark 14:30,72 — "Before the cock crow twice, thou shalt deny me thrice." Mark alone records the two cock-crows, a precise detail suggesting Peter's own eyewitness testimony as Mark's source.

kids.question: Who did the crowd choose to set free instead of Jesus?
kids.explanation: Mark 15:11,15 — "But the chief priests moved the people, that he should rather release Barabbas unto them... And so Pilate, willing to content the people, released Barabbas unto them."

kids.question: What did the soldiers put on Jesus' head?
kids.explanation: Mark 15:17 — "And they clothed him with purple, and platted a crown of thorns, and put it about his head."

kids.question: Who asked Pilate for Jesus' body to bury it?
kids.explanation: Mark 15:43 — "Joseph of Arimathaea, an honourable counsellor... went in boldly unto Pilate, and craved the body of Jesus."

teens.question: What did Pilate ask Jesus, and how did Jesus respond?
teens.option_a: Pilate asked "Art thou the King of the Jews?" and Jesus answered "Thou sayest it"
teens.option_b: Pilate asked "Are you guilty?" and Jesus said "No"
teens.option_c: Pilate asked "Do you know God?" and Jesus preached a sermon
teens.option_d: Pilate asked nothing and condemned Him immediately
teens.explanation: Mark 15:2 — "And Pilate asked him, Art thou the King of the Jews? And he answering said unto him, Thou sayest it."

teens.question: Who was compelled to carry Jesus' cross?
teens.option_a: Simon of Cyrene, the father of Alexander and Rufus
teens.explanation: Mark 15:21 — "And they compel one Simon a Cyrenian, who passed by, coming out of the country, the father of Alexander and Rufus, to bear his cross."

teens.question: What happened from the sixth hour to the ninth hour (noon to 3 PM)?
teens.option_a: There was darkness over the whole land
teens.option_d: The sun shone brighter than ever
teens.explanation: Mark 15:33 — "And when the sixth hour was come, there was darkness over the whole land until the ninth hour."

teens.question: What did Jesus cry out at the ninth hour?
teens.option_a: Eloi, Eloi, lama sabachthani — My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?
teens.option_d: Into thy hands I commend my spirit
teens.explanation: Mark 15:34 — "And at the ninth hour Jesus cried with a loud voice, saying, Eloi, Eloi, lama sabachthani? which is, being interpreted, My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?"

teens.question: What happened in the temple when Jesus died?
teens.option_a: The veil of the temple was rent in twain from the top to the bottom
teens.option_c: The doors of the temple opened
teens.option_d: The temple bells rang by themselves
teens.explanation: Mark 15:38 — "And the veil of the temple was rent in twain from the top to the bottom."

adults.question: What did Pilate marvel at regarding Jesus during the trial?
adults.option_a: That Jesus answered nothing to the many accusations against Him
adults.option_b: That Jesus spoke so eloquently
adults.option_c: That Jesus performed a miracle
adults.option_d: That Jesus was dressed in fine clothing
adults.explanation: Mark 15:5 — "But Jesus yet answered nothing; so that Pilate marvelled."

adults.question: What does the release of Barabbas (a murderer) instead of Jesus illustrate theologically?
adults.option_a: The innocent dying in the place of the guilty — a vivid picture of substitutionary atonement
adults.option_b: That the Jewish legal system was fair
adults.option_c: That Barabbas was innocent
adults.option_d: That crowds make good decisions
adults.explanation: Mark 15:7,15 — Barabbas, a murderer, was released while the innocent Jesus was condemned — a powerful illustration of the gospel: Christ takes the sinner's place.

adults.question: What is the significance of Jesus' cry "Eloi, Eloi, lama sabachthani?"
adults.option_a: It quotes Psalm 22:1, showing Jesus bore the full weight of divine judgment for sin, experiencing separation from the Father on behalf of humanity
adults.option_b: It shows Jesus lost faith on the cross
adults.option_c: It was an appeal to Elijah for help
adults.option_d: It was a prayer for physical relief
adults.explanation: Mark 15:34 — "My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?" quotes Psalm 22:1. Jesus experienced the spiritual agony of bearing the world's sin (2 Corinthians 5:21).

adults.question: What does the tearing of the temple veil from top to bottom signify?
adults.option_a: God Himself opened access to His presence — the barrier between God and man was removed through Christ's atoning death
adults.option_b: An earthquake caused structural damage
adults.option_c: The priests tore it in grief
adults.option_d: It was old and simply wore out
adults.explanation: Mark 15:38 — "And the veil of the temple was rent in twain from the top to the bottom." The direction (top to bottom) signifies divine action, not human; access to God is now open (Hebrews 10:19-20).

adults.question: What did the Roman centurion say when he saw how Jesus died?
adults.option_a: Truly this man was the Son of God
adults.option_b: This was just another criminal
adults.option_c: He deserved what he got
adults.option_d: His followers will cause trouble
adults.explanation: Mark 15:39 — "And when the centurion, which stood over against him, saw that he so cried out, and gave up the ghost, he said, Truly this man was the Son of God."

adults.question: Why is the centurion's confession in Mark 15:39 theologically significant?
adults.option_a: A Gentile soldier recognizes what Israel's leaders rejected — Jesus is the Son of God — echoing Mark 1:1 and forming an inclusio for the entire Gospel
adults.option_b: It shows that Roman soldiers were secret Christians
adults.option_c: It proves that execution methods were humane
adults.option_d: It demonstrates military respect for prisoners
adults.explanation: Mark 15:39 — "Truly this man was the Son of God." This confession by a Gentile bookends Mark's opening (1:1) and shows that the cross, not miracles, ultimately reveals Jesus' identity.

adults.question: Which women does Mark name as watching the crucifixion from afar?
adults.option_a: Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James the less and of Joses, and Salome
adults.option_b: Mary the mother of Jesus and Martha
adults.option_c: Joanna and Susanna
adults.option_d: Elizabeth and Anna
adults.explanation: Mark 15:40 — "There were also women looking on afar off: among whom was Mary Magdalene, and Mary the mother of James the less and of Joses, and Salome."

adults.question: What details does Mark provide about Joseph of Arimathaea that show the significance of his action?
adults.option_a: He was an honourable counsellor who also waited for the kingdom of God, went boldly to Pilate, bought fine linen, and laid Jesus in a hewn rock tomb
adults.option_b: He was a poor man with no resources
adults.option_c: He was a Roman citizen with political motives
adults.option_d: He was one of the twelve disciples
adults.explanation: Mark 15:43-46 — Joseph was a member of the Sanhedrin who risked his reputation and safety by publicly requesting Jesus' body for burial, fulfilling Isaiah 53:9.

kids.question: When the women came to the tomb, what did they find?
kids.option_a: The stone was rolled away and the tomb was empty
kids.explanation: Mark 16:4 — "And when they looked, they saw that the stone was rolled away: for it was very great."

kids.question: What did the young man in white tell the women?
kids.option_a: Jesus is risen; He is not here
kids.explanation: Mark 16:6 — "And he saith unto them, Be not affrighted: Ye seek Jesus of Nazareth, which was crucified: he is risen; he is not here."

kids.question: What did Jesus tell His disciples to do before He went to heaven?
kids.option_a: Go into all the world and preach the gospel to every creature
kids.explanation: Mark 16:15 — "And he said unto them, Go ye into all the world, and preach the gospel to every creature."

teens.question: Who came to the tomb very early on the first day of the week?
teens.option_a: Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James, and Salome
teens.explanation: Mark 16:1-2 — "And when the sabbath was past, Mary Magdalene, and Mary the mother of James, and Salome... came unto the sepulchre at the rising of the sun."

teens.question: What were the women worried about on the way to the tomb?
teens.option_a: Who shall roll us away the stone from the door of the sepulchre?
teens.option_b: Whether the soldiers would stop them
teens.option_d: Whether the tomb was the right one
teens.explanation: Mark 16:3 — "And they said among themselves, Who shall roll us away the stone from the door of the sepulchre?"

teens.question: To whom did Jesus first appear after His resurrection?
teens.option_a: Mary Magdalene, out of whom He had cast seven devils
teens.explanation: Mark 16:9 — "Now when Jesus was risen early the first day of the week, he appeared first to Mary Magdalene, out of whom he had cast seven devils."

teens.question: What did Jesus say would follow those who believe?
teens.option_a: Signs: casting out devils, speaking with new tongues, and taking up serpents without harm
teens.explanation: Mark 16:17 — "And these signs shall follow them that believe; In my name shall they cast out devils; they shall speak with new tongues."

teens.question: Where did Jesus go after speaking to His disciples?
teens.option_a: He was received up into heaven and sat on the right hand of God
teens.explanation: Mark 16:19 — "So then after the Lord had spoken unto them, he was received up into heaven, and sat on the right hand of God."

adults.question: What instructions did the young man in white give the women?
adults.option_a: Go tell His disciples and Peter that He goeth before you into Galilee; there shall ye see Him
adults.option_b: Stay here and wait for further instructions
adults.option_c: Go to the temple and offer sacrifice
adults.option_d: Return home and tell no one
adults.explanation: Mark 16:7 — "But go your way, tell his disciples and Peter that he goeth before you into Galilee: there shall ye see him, as he said unto you."

adults.question: Why is the specific mention of "and Peter" in verse 7 significant?
adults.option_a: It shows grace to the one who had denied Christ three times — Peter is personally named to assure him he is not excluded from restoration
adults.option_b: Peter was the leader so he naturally came first
adults.option_c: Peter lived the closest to the tomb
adults.option_d: It was an administrative instruction
adults.explanation: Mark 16:7 — "Tell his disciples and Peter." After his denials, Peter might have considered himself disqualified; the specific mention of his name is a message of personal grace and restoration.

adults.question: What is the textual significance of Mark 16:9-20?
adults.option_a: These verses (the "longer ending") are absent from the two oldest Greek manuscripts (Sinaiticus and Vaticanus) and are debated by scholars, though accepted in the KJV tradition
adults.option_b: All manuscripts contain these verses identically
adults.option_c: These verses were added in the 20th century
adults.option_d: Only Catholic manuscripts include them
adults.explanation: Mark 16:9-20 — The longer ending of Mark is present in the KJV and most manuscripts but absent from Codex Sinaiticus and Vaticanus. Scholars debate its originality.

adults.question: What does the Great Commission in Mark 16:15-16 emphasize compared to Matthew 28:19-20?
adults.option_a: Mark emphasizes believing and being baptized for salvation, with condemnation for unbelief — focusing on individual response to the gospel
adults.option_b: Mark focuses on making disciples of nations
adults.option_c: Mark emphasizes teaching commandments
adults.option_d: Mark is identical to Matthew's version
adults.explanation: Mark 16:15-16 — "He that believeth and is baptized shall be saved; but he that believeth not shall be damned." Mark's version stresses personal belief and its eternal consequences.

adults.question: How does the ending of Mark's Gospel at verse 8 create a literary effect if taken as the original ending?
adults.option_a: The abrupt ending — "for they were afraid" — leaves the reader to respond personally to the resurrection, creating an open-ended call to faith and action
adults.option_b: Mark simply ran out of papyrus
adults.option_c: The ending was accidentally lost
adults.option_d: Mark intended to write a sequel
adults.explanation: Mark 16:8 — "And they went out quickly, and fled from the sepulchre; for they trembled and were amazed: neither said they any thing to any man; for they were afraid." If original, this ending confronts the reader with the resurrection and demands a personal response.

adults.question: What does Jesus' ascension and sitting at the right hand of God signify?
adults.option_a: His exaltation, completion of His redemptive work, and present intercessory authority as King and High Priest
adults.option_b: He retired from active ministry
adults.option_c: He is waiting passively until the end
adults.option_d: He surrendered His power to the Father
adults.explanation: Mark 16:19 — "He was received up into heaven, and sat on the right hand of God." The right hand signifies authority and honor (cf. Psalm 110:1, Hebrews 1:3).

adults.question: What does the final verse of Mark say about the disciples' subsequent ministry?
adults.option_a: They went forth and preached every where, the Lord working with them and confirming the word with signs following
adults.option_b: They stayed in Jerusalem and never traveled
adults.option_c: They wrote books but did not preach
adults.option_d: They returned to their former occupations
adults.explanation: Mark 16:20 — "And they went forth, and preached every where, the Lord working with them, and confirming the word with signs following."

adults.question: How does Mark's Gospel as a whole present Jesus Christ?
adults.option_a: As the suffering Servant and Son of God — beginning with His divine identity (1:1), demonstrated through authoritative action, and climaxing in His sacrificial death and victorious resurrection
adults.option_b: Primarily as a moral teacher
adults.option_c: Only as a political revolutionary
adults.option_d: Mainly as a miracle worker with no deeper purpose
adults.explanation: Mark presents Jesus as the active, authoritative Son of God (1:1) who came to serve and give His life as a ransom for many (10:45), with His identity fully revealed through the cross (15:39).
